figures.gloss: The singular, formless, and genderless creator of the universe. Waheguru is the wondrous enlightener, completely immanent within all creation yet transcending it, devoid of incarnations, and eternal.
figures.facet: Waheguru vehemently rejects avatars or anthropomorphism. God does not descend as a human ('avatarvad'); rather, humans must ascend spiritually to merge with the divine, which is already within them.
figures.provenance: Japji Sahib (Mool Mantar); Guru Granth Sahib.

figures.gloss: The first Guru and founder of the Sikh path. He rejected caste, asceticism, and empty ritualism, traveling extensively to preach devotion to the One Creator and the absolute equality of all humanity.
figures.facet: He is not God incarnate, but a perfected human vessel for the divine light ('Jot'). His revelation was not about founding an exclusive tribe, but dismantling the oppressive orthodoxies of his time.

figures.gloss: The central religious scripture of Sikhism, regarded by Sikhs as the final, sovereign, and eternal living Guru. It contains the poetic hymns of the Sikh Gurus and various Hindu and Muslim saints.
figures.facet: It is treated not merely as a book, but as a living sovereign—given a royal canopy, resting quarters, and a fan ('chaur'). The text itself is the Guru, severing the need for any living human successor.
figures.provenance: Compiled by Guru Arjan Dev (Adi Granth, 1604); elevated by Guru Gobind Singh (1708).

figures.gloss: The false sense of self or ego-centeredness. It is the core spiritual disease that separates the human soul from the divine, manifesting as lust, wrath, greed, attachment, and pride (the 'Five Thieves').
figures.facet: Haumai is not a demonic entity or external devil; it is an internal psychological veil. It is both the primary cause of human suffering and the friction necessary for spiritual evolution.
figures.provenance: Guru Granth Sahib (passim, particularly Asa Di Var).

figures.gloss: The doctrine of intertwined temporal ('Miri') and spiritual ('Piri') authority, established by the sixth Guru, Hargobind. It mandates that a Sikh must be both a spiritually devoted saint and a politically engaged soldier.
figures.facet: It fundamentally rejects ascetic withdrawal from the world. Spiritual liberation and political liberation are inseparable; defending the oppressed is framed not as a secular necessity, but as a direct act of spiritual devotion.
figures.provenance: Akal Takht foundation (1606); early Sikh historical chronicles.

figures.gloss: The divine will or cosmic order that governs the universe. Recognizing, submitting to, and living in harmony with Hukam is the primary path to spiritual liberation and the cessation of the rebirth cycle.
figures.facet: Unlike arbitrary divine whim, Hukam is both the natural law governing physics and the moral law governing karma. Liberation is not changing God's mind via petitionary prayer, but aligning one's own will with the inevitable Hukam.

figures.gloss: The community kitchen in a Gurdwara where a free vegetarian meal is served to all visitors, without distinction of religion, caste, gender, economic status, or ethnicity.
figures.facet: It was specifically designed as a weapon against the caste system. High-caste and low-caste, king and peasant must sit on the exact same level ('pangat') and eat the same food, destroying ritual purity laws.
figures.provenance: Instituted by Guru Nanak; institutionalized by Guru Amar Das.

figures.gloss: The collective body of initiated Sikhs, forged by Guru Gobind Singh. Initiates wear the 'Five Ks', carry a sword, and pledge their lives to defend dharma, reject caste, and uphold justice.
figures.facet: It decentralized and transferred sovereignty to the collective laity. The Khalsa is the 'Guru-Panth' (the Guru manifest in the community), abolishing priestly classes and creating an egalitarian, armed citizenry of 'Saint-Soldiers'.
figures.provenance: Vaisakhi of 1699; Dasam Granth; Rehat Maryadas.

figures.gloss: Tenth and final human Guru (1666-1708); at Vaisakhi 1699 in Anandpur he called the Panj Piare from the crowd, initiated them with amrit, and refashioned Sikhs into the Khalsa, a casteless order bound by discipline and the sword. Before his death at Nanded he ended the line of living Gurus, investing eternal Guruship jointly in the Guru Granth Sahib and the Khalsa Panth.
figures.facet: He converted a persecuted community into a self-governing order by giving sovereignty away twice over — first to the collective initiated body (Guru Panth), then to the closed scripture (Guru Granth) — refusing to name a human successor even under his own dying breath.
figures.provenance: Bachittar Natak and other Dasam Granth compositions; Rehat Maryada; standard Sikh historiography (W.H. McLeod, Louis Fenech).

figures.gloss: Fifth Guru (1563-1606); compiled the Adi Granth in 1604 at Amritsar, gathering the hymns of his four predecessors, chosen Bhagats, and Sufi voices into a single authoritative text, and built the Harmandir Sahib as its seat. Arrested under Emperor Jahangir amid imperial succession politics, he died under torture at Lahore in 1606.
figures.facet: His death inaugurated Sikh martyrdom as a living category — not incidental persecution but a template later Gurus and the Khalsa itself would consciously invoke.
figures.provenance: Jahangir's memoir (Tuzuk-i-Jahangiri) records the execution; Adi Granth colophon tradition; Sikh historiography (Pashaura Singh, W.H. McLeod).

figures.gloss: Ninth Guru (1621-1675); when Kashmiri Pandits appealed for protection from forced conversion under Aurangzeb, he offered himself in their place, was arrested, and was beheaded in Delhi's Chandni Chowk in November 1675 after refusing to convert.
figures.facet: He died defending a faith not his own, making religious liberty itself — not doctrine, not territory — the cause worth martyrdom: the clearest enactment of Miri-Piri's fusion of spiritual and political duty.
figures.provenance: Bachittar Natak; Sikh historical chronicles (Bhatt Vahis); standard accounts (McLeod, Fenech, J.S. Grewal).

figures.gloss: The initiation rite instituted by Guru Gobind Singh at Vaisakhi 1699: amrit (sweetened water) stirred with a double-edged sword (khanda) to the recitation of five liturgical compositions, administered by five initiated Sikhs (the Panj Piare) to the new initiate, who drinks and is sprinkled with it.
figures.facet: It replaces birth and priestly ordination alike with a repeatable communal ceremony — Khalsa membership is manufactured, not inherited, by five ordinary initiated Sikhs acting with the Guru's own authority.
figures.provenance: Rehat Maryada (SGPC); Dasam Granth Vaisakhi narratives; Sikh historiography.

figures.gloss: The five articles of faith worn at all times by initiated (Amritdhari) Sikhs: kesh (uncut hair), kangha (wooden comb), kara (steel bracelet), kachera (cotton breeches), and kirpan (ceremonial sword) — instituted alongside the Khalsa's founding.
figures.facet: Faith made permanently visible on the body rather than confined to belief or occasional ritual — an initiated Sikh cannot pass as anything else, by design.
figures.provenance: Rehat Maryada; Dasam Granth-era Khalsa codes; Sikh studies scholarship (W.H. McLeod, Nikky-Guninder Kaur Singh).

figures.gloss: The devotional discipline of remembering and repeating the divine Name (most often 'Waheguru'), silently or aloud, as the primary daily practice of Sikh spiritual life, alongside reciting the set morning and evening liturgies (Nitnem).
figures.facet: It is the mechanism, not merely the goal: where Haumai is named as the disease, Naam Simran is the medicine actually taken, day by day, to align the self with Hukam.
figures.provenance: Guru Granth Sahib (recurring theme, e.g. Japji Sahib); Sikh devotional practice as documented across Sikh studies scholarship.

figures.gloss: The opening formula of the Guru Granth Sahib, composed by Guru Nanak: 'Ik Onkar, Sat Naam, Karta Purakh, Nirbhau, Nirvair, Akal Murat, Ajuni, Saibhang, Gur Prasad' — a compressed credal statement of the One, ever-true, uncreated, fearless, without enmity, timeless, unborn, self-existent, known by the Guru's grace.
figures.facet: Sikh liturgy treats this formula as generative rather than merely descriptive — reciting it is held to carry the entire theology of the Guru Granth Sahib in miniature, the seed every other hymn expands.
figures.provenance: Guru Granth Sahib, opening lines (Japji Sahib); standard translation and commentary (W.H. McLeod, Pashaura Singh).

figures.gloss: The Golden Temple at Amritsar, built at Guru Arjan's direction and completed around 1604 with the Adi Granth installed as its central object of reverence; the building has four doors opening to the four cardinal directions and sits below the surrounding street level, reached by a causeway across a sacred pool (Amrit Sarovar).
figures.facet: Its architecture argues the theology: four open doors and a floor set lower than the surrounding ground state, in stone, that no caste, creed, or rank stands above another at the threshold of the Guru's court.
figures.provenance: Madanjit Kaur, The Golden Temple: Past and Present; Sikh tradition on Guru Arjan's foundation.

figures.gloss: Selfless service performed without expectation of reward or recognition — cooking and serving in the langar, cleaning a Gurdwara, standing guard, or any labor undertaken for the community's benefit — treated as a direct form of worship.
figures.facet: It collapses the distinction between devotional and manual labor: scrubbing a floor and reciting scripture are held to be spiritually equivalent acts when performed as Seva.
figures.provenance: Guru Granth Sahib teachings on service; Sikh institutional practice (Rehat Maryada); Sikh studies scholarship (Eleanor Nesbitt).

figures.gloss: The standing congregational prayer that opens or closes Sikh ceremonies and most communal gatherings: it recalls the ten Gurus, invokes the Guru Granth Sahib, commemorates named Sikh martyrs and their sufferings, and closes with a plea for the well-being of all humankind ('sarbat da bhala').
figures.facet: Sikh identity is recited into memory at every Ardas — the prayer functions as a living roll call of the Gurus and the martyred, refusing to let the community's history soften into the merely doctrinal.
figures.provenance: Rehat Maryada (SGPC standard text); Sikh liturgical practice as documented in Sikh studies scholarship.

edges.note: Ik Onkar represents the absolute unity of all creation, whereas Haumai is the false illusion of separation and individual sovereignty.
The twin pillars of Sikh sovereignty after 1708: the eternal text ('Guru Granth') guiding the collective initiated community ('Guru Panth').

GOLD SEAM: The Sikh Gurus actively utilized the Vedantic vocabulary of a supreme, unmanifest reality ('Nirguna Brahman'), while radically reorienting it toward active, loving devotion rather than ascetic withdrawal.
GOLD SEAM: The Gurus frequently used Islamic names for God ('Allah', 'Khuda') interchangeably with Hindu names, asserting the absolute identity of the one formless creator worshipped by both.
GOLD SEAM: The infinite, formless, and genderless source of all reality that emanates into the universe without compromising its indivisible unity.
GOLD SEAM: The singular, supreme creator deity who is completely abstract, yet from whose formless will all the manifold universe vibrated into existence.
GOLD SEAM: A cosmic order that is simultaneously natural law and moral imperative, which the human soul must align with to achieve balance.
GOLD SEAM: The underlying flow of the universe; spiritual liberation is found not in fighting it or forcing it, but in effortless alignment with its current.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme adversary is not a literal demon, but the internal illusion of a separate self that keeps the soul trapped in the cycle of transmigration.
GOLD SEAM: While Zoroastrianism posits an external, co-eternal principle of evil, Sikhism rejects dualism entirely; evil is merely the internal sickness of the ego separating itself from the One.
GOLD SEAM: The inextricable unity and necessary balance of the spiritual/internal and the temporal/political realms.
GOLD SEAM: The philosophical pairing of the eternal, observing spirit with the active, material world, demanding mastery of both rather than the abandonment of the temporal.
GOLD SEAM: The sacralization of iron and the martial arts; the belief that righteous violence is a divine duty when all peaceful means of preserving justice have failed.
GOLD SEAM: A singular, physical object at the center of the faith that serves as the ultimate earthly focal point, vehemently distinguished from an idol, yet treated with supreme physical reverence.
GOLD SEAM: A radical, communal boiling of food that serves as both spiritual offering and social equalizer, erasing hierarchical boundaries in the act of sharing sustenance.
GOLD SEAM: The traveling prophet who actively crosses heavily guarded religious boundaries to synthesize a new, unifying revelation, rejecting the strictures of the competing orthodoxies.

GOLD SEAM: Radhasoami teachers expound the Adi Granth's shabd and nam hymns as their own vocabulary, and the Beas line was founded by the Sikh soldier Jaimal Singh — a documented drawing on Sikh scripture, not an endorsed one.

GOLD SEAM: the same guru-office resolved oppositely — Sikhism sealed guruship into the eternal text in 1708, while Sant Mat insists no text can initiate and reopened the living-master succession; the core reason orthodox Sikhism rejects Radhasoami.
GOLD SEAM: several hundred of Kabir's hymns and couplets stand in the Adi Granth (compiled 1604) as bhagat bani — a documented textual inclusion; the seam is bound into the book itself.
GOLD SEAM: Nanak stands in the same nirguna sant stream as Kabir — one formless God, caste rejected, devotion over ritual (McLeod's scholarly consensus); no meeting between the two is attested.

GOLD SEAM: The Guru Granth Sahib canonizes verses attributed to the Chishti master Shaykh Farid — a Sufi voice bound inside Sikh scripture; the inclusion is fact, though whether the hymns are Farid's own or his Pakpattan successors' is debated.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a scripture venerated not as words about the teaching but as the living embodiment and presence of the teaching itself.
Ravidas's hymns stand in the Adi Granth (compiled 1604) as bhagat bani, the same documented textual inclusion that carries Kabir's own verses.
Namdev's hymns likewise stand in the Adi Granth as bhagat bani, predating Kabir's own inclusion by more than a century of oral and manuscript transmission.
The Mool Mantar is the Guru Granth Sahib's opening credal statement of Ik Onkar — the formula naming the entity the whole scripture expounds.
Guru Arjan compiled the Adi Granth in 1604, the core text later sealed as the eternal Guru Granth Sahib.
Guru Gobind Singh closed the line of living Gurus in 1708, investing eternal Guruship in the Granth itself.
Guru Gobind Singh founded the Khalsa at Vaisakhi 1699, the direct historical act the roster's Khalsa entry describes.
Amrit Sanchar is the initiation ceremony that constitutes Khalsa membership — no Khalsa without it.
The Five Ks are the visible articles Guru Gobind Singh mandated for every initiated Khalsa Sikh.
Naam Simran is the devotional counter-practice that dissolves the ego Haumai names as the root spiritual disease.
Guru Tegh Bahadur's 1675 execution defending the Kashmiri Pandits' worship is Miri-Piri's fusion of spiritual and political duty enacted to the point of death.
Arjan's 1606 execution set the template of Sikh martyrdom under Mughal rule that Tegh Bahadur's 1675 execution followed.
Langar is Seva made edible and communal — the free kitchen is the ethic of selfless service performed as a standing institution.
The Harmandir Sahib was purpose-built as the Guru Granth Sahib's seat, housing its daily installation and court.
Ardas is recited at the close of readings from and ceremonies around the Guru Granth Sahib, the liturgical bracket to its daily court.
Both were executed by the state for refusing to recant a religious claim that threatened the existing order — structural kinship in founder-martyrdom, no historical link.

Both compress the entire practice into repeated invocation of a divine Name or Title — Waheguru's Naam against Nam-myoho-renge-kyo — independent traditions converging on repeated sacred utterance as the whole path.

dossier.essence: A monotheistic religion founded by Guru Nanak (1469–1539) in the Punjab and developed through a line of ten Gurus, holding that there is one formless, genderless creator (Ik Onkar, Waheguru) immanent in all things. With some 25–30 million adherents worldwide, it teaches liberation through devotion to the divine Name, honest labor, and service, and rejects caste, idolatry, empty ritual, and priestly hierarchy.
dossier.cosmos: One eternal, self-existent God creates and pervades a cosmos ordered by the divine will (hukam), and takes no incarnations — the aim is not that God descend but that the human ascend to merge with the divine already within. The soul passes through many births under karma; bound by egoism (haumai) and the 'five thieves,' it wins liberation by grace and by remembrance of the Name (nam simran), not by asceticism or withdrawal from the world.
dossier.practice: Worship centers on the gurdwara and the scripture itself — congregational singing of its hymns (kirtan), meditation on the Name, and the ethic of honest work and sharing. The free communal kitchen (langar), where all sit and eat together as equals, enacts the rejection of caste. Initiated members of the Khalsa, instituted by Guru Gobind Singh in 1699, keep the code of the Five Ks and the ideal of the 'saint-soldier,' binding spiritual devotion to the defense of justice (miri-piri).
dossier.canon: The Guru Granth Sahib — compiled by Guru Arjan as the Adi Granth (1604) and installed by Guru Gobind Singh in 1708 as the eternal Guru — gathering the hymns of the Sikh Gurus and of Hindu and Muslim saints; supplemented by the Dasam Granth and the Rahit codes of conduct.
dossier.reflects: The formless One approached through the Word — devotion fused with social equality, and liberation sought inside worldly and political life rather than in flight from it.
sources: Pashaura Singh & Louis E. Fenech (eds.), 'The Oxford Handbook of Sikh Studies' (2014)
Eleanor Nesbitt, 'Sikhism: A Very Short Introduction' (2005)
